nativeRange.habitat: Karstic limestone streams, sinkholes, and shallow ditches in Krabi Province, southern peninsular Thailand; clear, slow-moving to still water over rock and leaf litter, densely bordered by marginal vegetation including large beds of Cryptocoryne cordata in pools

decorPreferences: dense planting with Cryptocoryne cordata and other Cryptocoryne spp.
floating cover (Salvinia, Riccia, or tropical lily pads)
Indian almond leaf litter and driftwood for tannins and cover
clay pots or PVC tubes as spawning caves
tight-fitting lid with humid air gap above waterline

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on small live and frozen invertebrates: Daphnia, Artemia nauplii, chironomid (bloodworm) larvae, and micro-worms are all accepted. Zooplankton and small aquatic insects in the wild. Captive specimens can be conditioned onto high-quality dried micro-pellets and frozen foods, though live foods should remain a significant portion of the diet. Feed small amounts 1–2 times daily.

goodWith: small peaceful cyprinids from similar habitats (e.g., Trigonostigma spp.)
small loaches compatible with neutral-alkaline water
avoidWith: Betta splendens and other highly aggressive Betta spp.
fast-moving competitive feeders that outcompete slow hunters

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase reference image for Betta simplex; check for photographer credit and license before use.

summary: The Simplex Betta is a rare wild Betta from limestone karst streams in Krabi, Thailand. Unusually for the genus, it thrives in hard, alkaline water. Males are paternal mouthbrooders. It is listed as Critically Endangered by the IUCN, with a known range of under 10 km².
careNotes: Betta simplex belongs to the Betta picta species group and is one of the few Betta species adapted to hard, neutral-to-alkaline water — a direct reflection of its origins in calcium-rich limestone karst streams in Krabi Province, southern Thailand. Unlike most aquarium Bettas, soft or acidic water is unsuitable; aim for pH 7.0–8.0 with moderate hardness (gH 6–15). A tight-fitting lid is essential: like all anabantoids (fish that breathe atmospheric air through a specialized structure called the labyrinth organ), Betta simplex requires access to warm, humid air above the waterline, and cold drafts can cause respiratory problems. The tank should be heavily planted with Cryptocoryne species and floating plants such as Salvinia or Riccia, with Indian almond leaves and driftwood added for cover and refuge. Males tend to be less aggressive than many Betta species, though territorial disputes can still arise; a species-only setup or a carefully chosen community of small, peaceful cyprinids and loaches is recommended. Pairs or small groups can be kept given sufficient space and cover. Due to its Critically Endangered status and restricted wild range — the entire known area of occupancy is under 10 km² — hobbyist breeding to sustain captive populations is strongly encouraged. Avoid purchasing wild-caught specimens where captive-bred stock is available.
breedingNotes: Betta simplex is a paternal mouthbrooder. Females typically initiate spawning. After fertilisation, the male collects and holds the eggs in his buccal cavity for 9–16 days (typically around 10 days). The brooding male should ideally be moved to a separate, well-planted rearing tank to prevent disturbance; he will not eat during incubation. Fry are released as free-swimming, fully-formed juveniles capable of accepting motile micro-foods (micro-worms, newly hatched Artemia nauplii, infusoria) from day one. Brood sizes are relatively small compared to bubble-nest building Bettas. Conditioning both sexes on live foods prior to spawning significantly improves breeding success. Breeding difficulty is rated moderate due to the need for species-specific conditions and careful management of the brooding male.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Betta simplex

Provided adult size (45–60 mm SL), temperature (20–28°C), pH (7.0–8.0), hardness (54–215 ppm), minimum tank base (60 × 30 cm), diet (small live/frozen foods), native range (Krabi Province, Thailand), habitat (karstic limestone), breeding notes (paternal mouthbrooder, 9–16 day incubation), and compatibility guidance.

additional.notes: Specialist Betta hobbyist society resource. Confirmed paternal mouthbrooder, incubation 9–12 days (typically 10), female-initiated spawning, temperature 22–26°C, pH 7.0, dH 11, type locality (springs/ponds near National Highway 4034, northwest Krabi), IUCN Vulnerable (D2).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Krabi mouth-brooding betta
additional.notes: Confirmed max size (~8 cm), endemic range (Krabi Province, total area of occupancy under 10 km²), paternal mouthbrooder behavior, conservation threats (collection for aquarium trade, agricultural run-off, tourism development, eel-bait use). Listed IUCN Critically Endangered citing 2011 assessment.

additional.name: IUCN — ‘The planet will never see this species again’ (Betta simplex story)
additional.notes: IUCN editorial (August 2025) confirming critically endangered status and conservation pressures: over-exploitation for aquarium trade, eel-bait collection, agricultural chemical run-off, and tourism development in Krabi karst habitat.

fish.breedingNotes: Betta simplex is a paternal mouthbrooder. The female typically initiates spawning, after which the male collects and holds the fertilised eggs in his buccal cavity for 9–16 days. The brooding male should be moved to a quiet, separate rearing tank to minimize disturbance; he will not eat during incubation. Fry are released as free-swimming juveniles and accept motile micro-foods from the first day. Brood sizes are modest. Conditioning both sexes on live foods before spawning improves breeding success.
